text:
ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Medical Oncology — Melanoma Program

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
FELLOW/RESIDENT: [Fellow]
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient — Disease Monitoring Visit

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Marcus R. Ellison is a 57-year-old White male with BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV (T4b N3 M1c) malignant melanoma, primary site upper back, here for routine follow-up while on nivo (Opdivo) 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance monotherapy. This is his tenth cycle of Opdivo (cycle 10) on the maintenance schedule. He presents for toxicity review, clinical examination, labs, and restaging discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Ellison is a 57-year-old landscaper with a well-characterized disease course now spanning approximately 26 months since initial diagnosis. In brief: he was diagnosed with nodular melanoma of the upper back (Breslow 6.1 mm, ulcerated, Clark level V, mitotic rate 6/mm2) presenting de novo with Stage IV disease including bilateral axillary and left cervical lymphadenopathy, six hepatic metastases (largest 3.1 cm, segment VII), and multiple cutaneous/subcutaneous satellite nodules. LDH at diagnosis was 642 U/L (approximately 2x ULN). BRAF V600E mutation confirmed by both IHC (VE1 antibody, strongly positive) and next-generation sequencing (allele frequency 48.2% on 523-gene panel). PD-L1 TPS 25% / CPS 38.

First-line treatment was dabra (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus tram (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, chosen in the context of high disease burden and hepatic involvement with elevated LDH, favoring rapid disease control. He achieved an excellent early response — LDH dropped from 642 to 214 U/L by 6 weeks, with marked reduction in palpable disease. Restaging CT at 3 months confirmed robust partial response (right axillary mass 4.8 cm → 1.9 cm, hepatic segment VII 3.1 cm → 1.1 cm). He experienced a grade 2 cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cuSCC) of the right forearm at approximately 4 months — a recognized class-effect BRAF inhibitor adverse event — managed with wide local excision; no dose modification was required. Disease stabilized at the 6-month CT but progressed significantly by the 8-month restaging: right axillary mass grew back to 3.4 cm, the hepatic lesions re-expanded substantially, and new hepatic lesions (segments IV and VI) and a right external iliac node appeared. LDH surged to 589 U/L. Resistance biopsy confirmed retained BRAF V600E with newly emergent BRAF amplification (~8x copy number gain) and MAP2K1 p.P124S (MEK1 resistance mutation), along with a new PIK3CA p.E545K mutation — a pattern consistent with bypass of BRAF/MEK inhibition.

Tafinlar/tram was discontinued. After pre-immunotherapy brain MRI confirmed no CNS disease, he was transitioned to second-line combination immunotherapy: Yervoy (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus Opdivo (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks for 4 planned cycles (CheckMate 067 dosing). He tolerated cycles 1 and 2 with manageable grade 1-2 toxicities (fatigue, pruritus, maculopapular rash, grade 1 diarrhea). After cycle 3, however, he developed grade 3 immune-mediated colitis: 8-10 watery bowel movements per day, severe cramping, dehydration, fever to 38.6°C. He was hospitalized; sigmoidoscopy confirmed diffuse erosive colitis from rectum to splenic flexure; infectious workup including C. diff PCR was negative; biopsies showed marked cryptitis with crypt abscesses and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (CMV IHC negative). He was managed with IV methylprednisolone 2 mg/kg/day then oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, with infliximab 5 mg/kg x1 for incomplete steroid response at day 10. ipi was permanently discontinued given grade 3 severity. He improved substantially on infliximab and completed an extended steroid taper over approximately 8 weeks. Colonoscopy at 10 weeks post-onset confirmed mucosal healing. nivo was restarted as single-agent maintenance (480 mg q4 weeks) approximately 4 months after immunotherapy initiation, once prednisone was tapered to 5 mg/day and GI symptoms had been absent for at least 2 weeks.

Since resuming nivo monotherapy, he has shown an impressive and deepening treatment response. Restaging CT at approximately 5 months post-immunotherapy-start demonstrated significant partial response across all target lesions (right axillary mass 3.4 cm → 1.6 cm, hepatic segment VII 2.8 cm → 1.2 cm, segment II 1.6 cm → 0.7 cm). LDH normalized to 198 U/L. Follow-up clinical note at approximately 6 months documented ongoing response with no colitis recurrence, though TSH was mildly elevated at 5.8 mIU/L (subclinical hypothyroidism); levothyroxine 25 mcg/day was initiated and endocrinology was consulted. At approximately 8 months on immunotherapy, he developed overt grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism (TSH 14.2 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL) with symptoms of fatigue, constipation, weight gain, cold intolerance, and mild cognitive slowing; anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies were positive. Levothyroxine was uptitrated to 75 mcg/day; no corticosteroids were required. Repeat TSH at 6 weeks confirmed adequate replacement at 3.2 mIU/L, and symptoms resolved. Second restaging CT at approximately 9 months showed further deepening of response: right axillary mass 1.6 cm → 0.9 cm, hepatic segment VII 1.2 cm → 0.6 cm, segment II 0.7 cm → 0.4 cm; total target lesion sum reduced approximately 77% from the pre-immunotherapy baseline. No new lesions. Concurrent brain MRI surveillance was negative for CNS disease.

Today's visit is at approximately 10 months after immunotherapy start (cycle 10 of nivo maintenance). Mr. Ellison reports that he is doing extremely well overall. His energy has returned to near-normal. He has resumed a modified work schedule with his landscaping business (light duty). He denies any diarrhea, abdominal cramping, or GI complaints. No rash. No dyspnea. No chest pain or palpitations. He feels that his fatigue, which had been a persistent grade 1 complaint throughout much of his treatment course, has now largely resolved. He reports no fevers, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. He has noted mild and stable constipation, which he attributes in part to his levothyroxine therapy and which is managed with dietary modification and occasional stool softener use. He denies any new skin lesions, and he has been keeping up with his monthly dermatology surveillance visits as recommended. He has not noted any visual changes, headaches, focal neurologic deficits, or signs that would raise concern for CNS involvement.

He takes levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (AM, on empty stomach) for immune-mediated hypothyroidism and prednisone, now tapered to 2.5 mg once daily. He is tolerant of his current regimen and has no new medication concerns.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Fatigue significantly improved. No fever. No night sweats. No weight loss (stable weight). Mild cold intolerance, improved on levothyroxine.
Gastrointestinal: No diarrhea. Bowel movements 1/day, formed. Mild constipation managed with dietary fiber. No hematochezia. No rectal urgency. No nausea or vomiting. No abdominal pain.
Skin/Integument: No new skin lesions identified since last dermatology visit. No rash. No pruritus. Prior cuSCC site (right forearm) well-healed per dermatology report.
Endocrine: Hypothyroidism stable and well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg. No symptoms of adrenal insufficiency (no fatigue worsening, no hypotension, no hyponatremia-related symptoms). Appetite normal.
Neurologic: No headaches. No visual changes. No cognitive changes (prior mild cognitive slowing with hypothyroidism fully resolved). No focal weakness or sensory deficits.
Respiratory: No cough. No dyspnea on exertion. No hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain. No palpitations. No lower extremity edema.
Musculoskeletal: No joint pain or swelling. No myalgias.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria, or urinary symptoms.
Hematologic: No easy bruising. No epistaxis.

---

CURRENT MEDICATIONS
1. Opdivo (Opdivo) 480 mg IV every 4 weeks — cycle 10 today
2. Levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg PO daily (AM, fasting)
3. Prednisone 2.5 mg PO daily (ongoing slow taper from grade 3 colitis management — near-physiologic dose)
4. Omeprazole 20 mg PO daily (GI prophylaxis, continued from colitis management period)
5. Docusate sodium 100 mg PO daily PRN (mild constipation)
6. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO PRN (occasional use)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished male in no acute distress. Alert and fully oriented. Appears stated age. No distress. Pleasant and conversational.

Vitals:
  - Blood pressure: 122/76 mmHg
  - Heart rate: 68 bpm, regular
  - Temperature: 36.9°C (oral)
  - Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
  - O2 saturation: 98% on room air
  - Weight: 84.2 kg (stable from last visit, prior weight loss with hypothyroidism now fully corrected)
  - BMI: 26.8 kg/m2
  - ECOG Performance Status: 0

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear without mucositis. No scleral icterus. Pupils equal and reactive bilaterally. No temporal tenderness.

Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical lymphadenopathy. Prior palpable left cervical level II/III nodes are no longer detectable on careful examination. No thyromegaly. Trachea midline.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or rales. Normal chest excursion.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. S1 and S2 normal. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Bowel sounds present in all four quadrants. No hepatomegaly by percussion or palpation. No splenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. No guarding or rigidity. No rebound tenderness. (Abdomen previously notable for prior subcutaneous nodules on trunk — none currently palpable.)

Skin: Careful full-torso skin survey performed. Well-healed excision scar right forearm from prior cuSCC resection. No suspicious pigmented lesions noted on trunk or visible extremities. No maculopapular rash. No palmar hyperkeratosis (resolved, previously grade 1 on Tafinlar). Skin turgor normal. No jaundice.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary lymphadenopathy bilaterally. The previously prominent right axillary nodal region — which at progression had included a 3.4 cm conglomerate mass — now has no palpable discrete lymphadenopathy. There is a small area of subtle firmness in the right axillary fossa, likely fibrotic residual tissue, consistent with the small 0.9 cm residual soft tissue seen on prior CT. No inguinal, femoral, popliteal, or epitrochlear lymphadenopathy.

Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion in all major joints. No point tenderness over spine or long bones.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal. Coordination intact.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
  - WBC: 6.4 x10^3/uL (normal)
  - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (improved from prior 11.4 g/dL; resolving grade 1 anemia)
  - Hematocrit: 38.9%
  - Platelets: 218 x10^3/uL
  - Neutrophils: 4.1 x10^3/uL
  - Lymphocytes: 1.8 x10^3/uL
  - Monocytes: 0.4 x10^3/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - Sodium: 139 mEq/L
  - Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
  - Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
  - BUN: 14 mg/dL
  - Creatinine: 0.94 mg/dL (eGFR >60)
  - Glucose: 98 mg/dL
  - Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL
  - AST: 28 U/L (normal)
  - ALT: 31 U/L (normal)
  - Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
  - Total protein: 7.1 g/dL
  - Albumin: 4.2 g/dL

LDH: 148 U/L (WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS; normal range 120-246 U/L at our institution)
  — Notable trajectory: 642 U/L at diagnosis → 214 U/L at 6 weeks on Tafinlar/Mekinist → nadir 167 U/L on BRAF/MEK → 589 U/L at progression → 412 U/L after 2 cycles of ipi/nivo → 198 U/L at 5-month restaging → 152 U/L at 9-month restaging → 148 U/L today.

Thyroid Function:
  - TSH: 2.8 mIU/L (normal range 0.4-4.0; well-controlled on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
  - Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)
  — Adequate thyroid hormone replacement confirmed. No dose change needed at this time.

Endocrine / Immune-Related Toxicity Monitoring:
  - AM cortisol: 14.8 mcg/dL (adequate; no evidence of adrenal insufficiency)
  - Testosterone (checked for fatigue workup): 312 ng/dL (low-normal, consistent with age and prior inflammatory burden; not action-warranting at this time)
  - CBC differential: no eosinophilia
  - CRP: 3.1 mg/L (mildly elevated but trending down from prior; likely residual inflammatory state)

Urinalysis: Clear, no proteinuria, no hematuria.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Most recent imaging reviewed today: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast from approximately 1 month ago (second restaging on immunotherapy, at approximately 9 months post-start), as well as brain MRI from the same timeframe — both reviewed and discussed:

CT CAP (9-month restaging): Deepening partial response across all previously measurable target lesions. The right axillary residual soft tissue is now 0.9 cm — down from the prior 3.4 cm at progression nadir. Hepatic lesions have continued to shrink: the segment VII lesion is now only 0.6 cm, essentially near the resolution threshold. The segment II lesion measures 0.4 cm and approaches the limit of measurability. The previously new segment IV hepatic lesion resolved entirely. No new lesions anywhere. Right external iliac node is now 0.4 cm and back within normal size range. LDH corroborates the imaging response.

Brain MRI (9-month surveillance): No intracranial metastases identified. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal study.

Note: A PET-CT has been discussed with the patient and will be ordered to further characterize metabolic activity of the small residual lesions. This imaging has not yet been performed at the time of today's visit but has been requested and will be scheduled.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Marcus Ellison is a 57-year-old male with metastatic BRAF V600E-mutant Stage IV nodular melanoma (M1c, non-CNS), now approximately 10 months into immunotherapy (roughly 26 months from initial diagnosis). He is on Opdivo (Opdivo) 480 mg IV every 4 weeks maintenance monotherapy following inability to complete combination Yervoy/nivo due to grade 3 immune-mediated colitis after cycle 3 (ipi permanently discontinued). He is tolerating Opdivo monotherapy well and showing an excellent, durable, deepening response.

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA — NEAR-COMPLETE RESPONSE ON nivo MONOTHERAPY

Mr. Ellison has had a remarkable overall treatment response. After rapid initial response to dabra/Mekinist followed by acquired resistance at 8 months, he transitioned to checkpoint immunotherapy. Despite early complicated by grade 3 colitis limiting Yervoy to 3 cycles, he has achieved deep and ongoing tumor control on Opdivo alone. His disease trajectory — with LDH now at 148 U/L (its lowest recorded point), nearly all target lesions reduced to near-resolution on imaging, and no palpable lymphadenopathy or subcutaneous nodules — is highly encouraging.

Plan:
- Administer nivo 480 mg IV today (cycle 10). Pre-medications per standard protocol. Infusion over 30 minutes.
- Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months (4-month imaging interval agreed upon given depth of response and stability of residual lesions).
- PET-CT (18F-FDG, whole body) ordered now to fully characterize the metabolic activity of residual soft tissue in the right axilla and any residual hepatic signals. This will help distinguish truly resolved disease from fibrotic/necrotic residual and will inform future treatment duration discussions.
- Brain MRI to be repeated in approximately 2-3 months (6-month interval from last MRI).
- Continue Opdivo indefinitely per tumor board recommendation given M1c disease, initial high volume hepatic involvement, and risk of occult residual disease. Duration of Opdivo therapy will be revisited as data matures; no treatment holiday planned at this time.

2. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM — STABLE, WELL-CONTROLLED

Grade 2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism diagnosed approximately 8 months into immunotherapy, treated with levothyroxine uptitrated to 75 mcg/day. Today's TSH 2.8 mIU/L — excellent control. Free T4 normal. Patient asymptomatic from a thyroid standpoint.

Plan:
- Continue levothyroxine (Synthroid) 75 mcg PO daily.
- Recheck TSH and free T4 in approximately 3 months (thyroid monitoring every 3 months ongoing per protocol).
- Endocrinology aware; follow-up appointment scheduled independently. Likely permanent thyroid dysfunction will require lifelong levothyroxine replacement.

3. STEROID TAPER — NEAR COMPLETION

Patient remains on prednisone 2.5 mg/day — essentially near-physiologic, the residual tail of the long steroid taper initiated for grade 3 colitis. He has been on this dose for approximately 2 months without symptoms of colitis recurrence. AM cortisol 14.8 mcg/dL is adequate, suggesting the adrenal axis is recovering appropriately and that further taper should be safe.

Plan:
- Taper prednisone to 2.5 mg every other day for 2-4 weeks, then discontinue if well tolerated and AM cortisol repeats normal.
- Counsel patient regarding adrenal insufficiency warning signs at very low/stopping doses (increased fatigue, dizziness, nausea). Provide medic-alert information.
- If asymptomatic and cortisol adequate, anticipate complete steroid discontinuation at next visit.
- No evidence of colitis recurrence; no GI symptoms. Continue omeprazole while on residual prednisone and taper off once prednisone is fully discontinued.

4. GASTROINTESTINAL MONITORING POST-GRADE 3 IMMUNE COLITIS

Patient remains in complete remission from his grade 3 immune-mediated colitis. Healing confirmed by colonoscopy at 10 weeks post-event. No current GI symptoms.

Plan:
- Continue monitoring for any recurrence of GI symptoms at each visit; patient is well-educated on warning signs.
- Surveillance colonoscopy deferred given ongoing asymptomatic status and confirmed mucosal healing on prior endoscopy.
- If new GI symptoms develop (diarrhea >3 stools/day above baseline, cramping, hematochezia), hold Opdivo and proceed with urgent GI evaluation per standard irAE protocols.
- Low-residue dietary counseling reinforced.

5. DERMATOLOGIC SURVEILLANCE

History of grade 2 cuSCC right forearm (excised approximately 18 months ago) as a class-effect BRAF inhibitor complication. Risk of additional keratoacanthomas or squamous cell carcinomas remains relevant, particularly on sun-exposed sites. Patient is a landscaper with occupational UV exposure history.

Plan:
- Continue monthly full-body skin examinations with dermatology.
- Patient counseled to minimize sun exposure, use broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen daily, wear protective clothing and wide-brim hat during occupational sun exposure.
- Annual total-body photography with dermatology to assist in longitudinal monitoring.

6. ANEMIA — RESOLVING

Prior grade 1 anemia (hemoglobin nadir 11.4 g/dL), attributed to multifactorial causes including chronic inflammation, prior therapy, and immune dysregulation. Today's hemoglobin 13.1 g/dL — essentially back to normal range. Iron studies not formally repeated today given trend toward normalization.

Plan:
- No specific intervention required. Monitor CBC at each visit.
- If hemoglobin drops again, consider further workup (iron studies, reticulocyte count, direct Coombs for immune-mediated hemolytic anemia — rare but documented with checkpoint inhibitor therapy).

7. HYPOTHALAMIC-PITUITARY-ADRENAL AND OTHER IMMUNE-RELATED TOXICITY MONITORING

No current clinical evidence of hypophysitis, adrenalitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, nephritis, or myocarditis. AM cortisol 14.8 mcg/dL is reassuring against adrenal insufficiency.

Plan:
- Obtain liver function tests (done today, all normal), CBC (done today, normal), TSH/free T4 (done today, normal), AM cortisol (done today, adequate) at each Opdivo visit.
- Low threshold for echocardiogram if new dyspnea or chest symptoms arise (immune-mediated myocarditis is rare but potentially life-threatening).
- Testosterone noted to be low-normal (312 ng/dL); will recheck at next visit. No symptoms of hypogonadism beyond that which is age-appropriate and post-inflammatory. No immediate intervention planned.

8. CNS SURVEILLANCE

Brain MRI most recently negative at approximately 9 months. Patient remains asymptomatic from a neurologic standpoint.

Plan:
- Next brain MRI with and without contrast in approximately 2-3 months (targeting 6-month interval surveillance per institutional protocol).
- Prompt evaluation if any new neurologic symptoms arise.

9. QUALITY OF LIFE AND RETURN TO FUNCTION

Mr. Ellison has returned to a modified work schedule and reports excellent quality of life. He is highly engaged in his care, adherent to all medications and surveillance appointments, and well-informed about his diagnosis and treatment trajectory.

Plan:
- Social work and patient navigation support offered; patient declines at this time, feeling supported by family.
- Discuss with patient: while current data show approximately 20% of patients treated with ipi/nivo-based regimens achieve durable long-term remission, his current trajectory is very favorable. Conversations about potential future treatment de-escalation or holiday will be guided by long-term imaging results and tumor board input.
- Exercise counseling: encouraged to continue light-to-moderate physical activity as tolerated; aerobic exercise has demonstrated quality-of-life benefit in melanoma survivors and may modulate immune function favorably.
- Provide written summary of today's visit and updated medication list.

Follow-up: Return to clinic in 4 weeks for cycle 11 of Opdivo and interim clinical assessment. Formal restaging CT and PET-CT results will be reviewed at that time or at next scheduled restaging visit.

Time spent: Approximately 40 minutes, more than half in counseling and coordination of care.

[Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist, Melanoma Program]
[Fellow co-signature pending]
